title: Evaluating large language models for accuracy incentivizes hallucinations
core_conclusion: LLM hallucinations are a structural incentive problem, not merely a training deficiency. Next-word pretraining creates unavoidable statistical pressure toward guessing for rare singleton facts (proven via IIV reduction and singleton-rate lower bound), and dominant accuracy-based evaluations reward confident wrong answers over honest abstention. Open rubric evaluations — which explicitly state error penalties — realign incentives and allow frontier models to adopt hallucination-reduction mitigations without sacrificing headline accuracy scores.
why_it_matters: Reframes hallucination as mechanism design rather than engineering failure; provides a formal learning-theory lower bound on unavoidable hallucination; demonstrates a zero-extra-cost evaluation fix (open rubrics) validated on four frontier models; meta-evaluation reveals the majority of widely cited benchmarks structurally reward guessing.
why_nature: Theory plus large-scale experiment in one work (formal proofs + 4,326-question empirical validation); universal incentive-misalignment diagnosis applies across all LLMs and benchmarks; societal urgency in reliability-critical domains (medical, legal, scientific); open-source code and reusable evaluation framework constitute an infrastructure contribution.
added_value_to_my_research: Open-rubric evaluation framework for geospatial LLM benchmarks — aligns spatial fact accuracy with real cost of spatial errors in healthcare access and facility routing
Singleton-rate hallucination audit tool — diagnose training data gaps in domain-specific geospatial corpora before fine-tuning foundation models
Abstention-aware RLHF reward signal — train geospatial LLMs to abstain when spatial evidence is thin, improving R2SFCA pipeline reliability
Open-rubric RAG evaluation — fairly compare retrieval-augmented spatial query systems that must decide when to abstain given heterogeneous multi-source evidence
LLM-based healthcare access tool audit — apply meta-evaluation framework to clinical decision support benchmarks, reducing harm from spatial misinformation in patient routing

related.title: Detecting hallucinations in large language models using semantic entropy
related.relation: complementary hallucination detection method using semantic consistency rather than evaluation-design intervention

related.title: Optimizing generative AI by backpropagating language model feedback
related.relation: differentiable feedback framework through which open-rubric reward signals could be propagated during RLHF training

related.title: Training large language models on narrow tasks can lead to broad misalignment
related.relation: narrow fine-tuning concentrates singletons and amplifies distributional shift, compounding singleton-rate hallucination risk

related.title: Synthesizing scientific literature with retrieval-augmented language models
related.relation: RAG pipeline whose reliability could be benchmarked under open-rubric evaluation to test abstention under low-evidence retrieval

related.title: A roadmap for evaluating moral competence in large language models
related.relation: parallel evaluation-design agenda for LLM competence assessment, shares the critique that binary accuracy metrics miss important model behaviors

figures.caption: Origins and persistence of hallucination across training and evaluation — pretraining pressure, alignment, and accuracy-based evaluation collectively incentivize guessing
Generative errors as IIV misclassification — three error regimes (spelling, counting, arbitrary facts) mapped onto the binary classifier view
Accuracy as a barrier to hallucination reduction — consistency-based mitigation cuts incorrect responses but lowers accuracy (n = 4,326 per model)
